structured_choices.data_source: Multi-hospital health system inpatient EHR data spanning 4 years (48 months). Includes all hospitalizations with complete discharge records, linked to 90-day follow-up readmission outcomes via claims data and EHR re-encounter records. Exclusions: patients with unknown discharge destination, missing core admission/discharge timestamps, or no valid insurance/follow-up linkage during the 90-day window.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary outcome: unplanned readmission to any acute care bed within 90 calendar days of discharge. Admissions for planned procedures (identified via admission type codes and prior scheduling flags) are excluded. Patients experiencing in-hospital death before discharge are excluded from the cohort. Patients with loss-to-follow-up (e.g., insurance disenrollment) or who die within 90 days post-discharge are censored and removed from analysis.
structured_choices.split_method: Chronological (temporal) split. Training cohort: all discharges occurring in months 1–24. Validation cohort: all discharges in months 25–30 (used for hyperparameter selection and early stopping). Test cohort: all discharges in months 31–36. A held-out temporal buffer of 6 months (months 37–42) is retained unused to assess whether model performance degrades on completely fresh data and to protect against drift. Justification: This hypothesis concerns longitudinal modeling of EHR histories; temporal splitting prevents the model from encoding information about post-discharge events (outcomes, readmissions, or discharge practices) into training features. Chronological order preserves realistic deployment conditions where the model predicts on newly discharged patients using only their discharge-time information.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (standardization, missing value imputation strategies, categorical encoding mappings, and RUC-based feature selection thresholds) are fit exclusively on the training cohort (months 1–24). These fitted transformations are then applied identically to validation and test cohorts. This prevents validation and test data from influencing feature scaling or imputation strategies, which would bias downstream evaluation.
structured_choices.features: Two feature groups are constructed: (1) Baseline features: age, sex, admission diagnoses (primary and secondary, encoded as binary indicators for comorbidity groups using Elixhauser classification), and admission source. (2) Longitudinal event history features extracted from the 365 days prior to admission: (a) utilization summaries—count and recency of prior ED visits, inpatient admissions, and outpatient encounters in rolling windows (30, 90, 365 days); (b) diagnosis prevalence—binary indicators for chronic conditions observed in prior encounters and their temporal consistency (proportion of prior encounters mentioning each diagnosis); (c) medication refill patterns—counts of distinct medications refilled in prior 90 days, changes in medication counts, and indicators of lapsed refills (gaps >60 days); (d) lab test recency and abnormality flags for key values (e.g., creatinine, hemoglobin) in prior 180 days. All timestamps are computed relative to discharge time; no post-discharge information is included. Missing values in longitudinal features (e.g., no prior labs) are imputed with domain-specific codes (e.g., 'never observed') rather than zeros to preserve distinguishability.
structured_choices.model: Survival model: Cox proportional hazards regression with elastic net regularization (L1 + L2 penalty, tuned on validation set). This model directly accommodates censoring and time-to-event structure. Alternatively, a parametric survival model (Weibull or log-normal AFT) may be trained in parallel to assess robustness. The model outputs a continuous risk score (linear predictor) for each patient, which is converted to a probability of readmission by 90 days using the survival function estimated on the training set.
structured_choices.baseline: Static logistic regression trained on admission-time features only: age, sex, primary admission diagnosis, comorbidities (Elixhauser), and admission source. No longitudinal history features are included. The baseline model is tuned with equal effort: L2 regularization hyperparameter is selected via grid search on the validation set using the same primary metric (see below). Both baseline and proposed model receive identical preprocessing, train/val/test splits, and evaluation protocol. This ensures the comparison isolates the effect of longitudinal features and the survival framework, not differences in development rigor.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Harrell's C-index (concordance statistic) computed on the test set. This metric measures ranking calibration: the probability that the model correctly orders risk among pairs of patients (one who was readmitted, one who was not within 90 days). C-index is chosen because (1) it respects censoring and time-to-event data; (2) it directly reflects operational value—the hospital's ability to rank and prioritize patients for intervention resources depends on correct relative risk ordering, not absolute probability calibration; (3) it is threshold-agnostic, avoiding artificial dependency on an intervention threshold that may vary by hospital capacity. Minimum clinically meaningful improvement: Δ C-index ≥ 0.03 (corresponding to approximately 3 percentage point absolute improvement in pairwise discrimination).
secondary_metrics: Calibration-in-the-large (slope and intercept of calibration plot on test set) to assess whether predicted probabilities align with observed 90-day readmission rates in decile bins
Sensitivity and specificity at the 50th percentile risk threshold to assess performance on a common decision boundary
Positive predictive value (PPV) and negative predictive value (NPV) in the top 20% risk stratum, to reflect intervention targeting
C-index stratified by prior utilization intensity (high vs. low) to test the hypothesis that longitudinal features especially benefit irregular utilization patients
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning (L1/L2 regularization weights, elastic net mix parameter) is performed via 5-fold cross-validation within the training set. The fold structure is stratified by readmission rate (binary) and by hospital site to ensure balanced representation. Validation set (months 25–30) is used for early stopping and final model selection: the regularization configuration that maximizes Harrell's C-index on the validation set is retained. The test set is locked and used only once, at the end, to report final performance.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set (months 31–36) is not accessed during model development, hyperparameter search, or feature engineering. Performance is reported in full (C-index and secondary metrics) as a single final evaluation. No model retraining or threshold adjustment occurs after test set evaluation. The held-out temporal buffer (months 37–42) is used to re-evaluate the test-set-trained model on an independent cohort to quantify any performance drift; this is reported separately and does not influence the primary hypothesis test.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Length of stay (LOS) and discharge intensity proxies (e.g., count of medications at discharge, count of discharge diagnoses) are included as features in both the proposed and baseline models. This isolates longitudinal history information from discharge practice artifacts. Competing risk of death is handled via censoring: patients who die post-discharge are removed and do not count as readmissions or negatives. To test whether improvements are driven by the longitudinal representation specifically (not just model complexity), an intermediate model—logistic regression with the same longitudinal features—is also trained; if it matches or exceeds the Cox model, the improvement is primarily feature-driven, not survival-structure-driven. Hospital site and service type (e.g., medical vs. surgical) are included as model features to adjust for site-level discharge practices. Post-hoc subgroup analysis compares C-index improvement in high-irregularity vs. low-irregularity patients (stratified by interquartile range of prior ED/admission frequency) to confirm the hypothesis targets the intended population.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Longitudinal EHR Survival Model for 90-Day Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data Cohort and Outcome Definition

The experiment uses 4 years of inpatient EHR data from a multi-hospital health system, including all hospitalizations with complete admission/discharge timestamps, diagnoses, procedures, medications, and laboratory results. The outcome is binary: unplanned readmission to any acute care bed within 90 calendar days of discharge. Admissions for planned procedures (identified via admission type coding and pre-scheduled status) are excluded. Patients who die in-hospital are excluded from the analysis; those who die within 90 days post-discharge are censored and removed (not counted as negative outcomes). Loss-to-follow-up (e.g., insurance disenrollment) is handled by removal. This protocol ensures the label reflects true clinical readmission risk conditional on surviving to discharge and remaining observable.

### 2. Temporal Split and Leakage Prevention

The data is split chronologically, not randomly: training cohort (months 1–24), validation cohort (months 25–30), and test cohort (months 31–36). A 6-month buffer (months 37–42) is retained unused to assess performance degradation on fully prospective data. Temporal splitting is essential because the hypothesis involves longitudinal modeling—the model must not encode any information about post-discharge outcomes or discharge practices that occur after the prediction window. Chronological splitting guarantees that all post-discharge readmissions in training and validation are observed before test data are collected, preventing data leakage and preserving realistic deployment conditions.

### 3. Feature Construction

Two feature categories are engineered:

**Baseline features** (admission-time snapshot): age, sex, primary and secondary admission diagnoses (Elixhauser comorbidity grouping), and admission source (ED, transfer, direct admission).

**Longitudinal event history features** (365 days prior to admission): (a) utilization intensity—counts and recency of prior ED visits, inpatient admissions, and outpatient encounters in rolling 30-day, 90-day, and 365-day windows; (b) diagnosis consistency—binary indicators for chronic conditions observed in prior encounters and the fraction of prior encounters mentioning each diagnosis; (c) medication dynamics—count of distinct medications refilled in the prior 90 days, recent changes in medication count, and flags for lapsed refills (gaps >60 days); (d) laboratory recency and abnormality—binary flags for availability and abnormality of key labs (creatinine, hemoglobin, glucose) within 180 days. All timestamps are anchored to discharge; no information from post-discharge is included. Missing values (e.g., patients with no prior lab draws) are encoded as categorical indicators rather than zeros, preserving clinical distinctiveness.

All preprocessing transformers (standardization, missing value imputation, categorical encoding) are fit exclusively on the training cohort and then applied identically to validation and test cohorts. This prevents the validation and test data from influencing feature distributions.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The proposed model is a **Cox proportional hazards regression with elastic net regularization** (combined L1 and L2 penalty). This model accommodates censoring and time-to-event structure naturally, producing a continuous risk score that is converted to a 90-day readmission probability using the baseline survival function from the training set.

The baseline is a **static logistic regression** trained only on admission-time features (age, sex, admission diagnosis, comorbidities, admission source)—no longitudinal history. The baseline receives equal tuning effort: L2 regularization is selected via grid search on the validation set using the same primary metric as the proposed model. Both models are evaluated under identical conditions, ensuring the comparison isolates the value of longitudinal history and the survival framework.

### 5. Primary Metric and Clinical Justification

The primary metric is **Harrell's C-index** (concordance statistic), computed on the test set. C-index measures the probability that the model correctly ranks risk between pairs of patients—one readmitted, one not. This metric directly reflects operational value: hospitals have limited intervention capacity (transitional care, follow-up appointments, medication reconciliation) and must identify the highest-risk patients to prioritize. Correct ranking is the essential requirement; absolute probability calibration, while important, is secondary to discrimination. C-index also naturally handles censoring and respects the time-to-event structure. A clinically meaningful improvement is defined as Δ C-index ≥ 0.03 (3 percentage points).

### 6. Secondary Metrics

Secondary metrics provide fuller context: (1) Calibration-in-the-large (slope and intercept of calibration plots and decile-binned observed vs. predicted rates) to verify predicted probabilities align with true readmission frequencies; (2) sensitivity and specificity at standard risk thresholds; (3) positive and negative predictive values in the top 20% risk stratum, reflecting real intervention targeting; (4) C-index stratified by prior utilization intensity (high vs. low), to confirm the model benefits the irregular-utilization subgroup hypothesized to benefit most.

### 7. Model Selection and Validation Protocol

Hyperparameter tuning (elastic net L1/L2 weights, mixing parameter) is performed via 5-fold cross-validation within the training cohort, with folds stratified by readmission rate and hospital site. The validation set (months 25–30) is used to select the final regularization configuration by maximizing C-index; this configuration is then applied to the test set without any further tuning. The test set is locked and evaluated only once at the end, reported in full.

### 8. Confound Control and Robustness Checks

Length of stay and discharge intensity (medication and diagnosis counts at discharge) are included as features in both proposed and baseline models, isolating longitudinal history signal from discharge practice artifacts. Competing risk of death is managed via censoring. An intermediate model—logistic regression trained on the same longitudinal features—is also trained; if it matches the Cox model's performance, the improvement is feature-driven, not survival-framework-driven. Hospital site and service type are included as model features to adjust for site-level discharge practice variation. Finally, post-hoc subgroup analysis compares C-index improvement between high- and low-irregularity patients to confirm the hypothesis targets the intended population and is not an artifact of overall model complexity.

### 9. Scope and Interpretation

This design will establish whether a longitudinal survival model outperforms a static baseline on a held-out temporal test set and whether this improvement is greatest in high-irregularity patients. It will not establish causality or generalization beyond this health system without external validation; nor will it evaluate the operational impact of deploying the model (e.g., intervention uptake, cost-effectiveness). The temporal split and confound controls are designed to support a claim of improved predictive ranking, not clinical benefit or optimality of the intervention strategy.
